title: Hadoop
description: An open-source framework that allows for the distributed processing of large data sets across clusters of computers.
keyPoints: Fault-tolerant and highly available
Highly scalable
Uses MapReduce programming model for processing

title: HDFS
description: The Hadoop Distributed File System is the primary data storage system used by Hadoop. It distributes data across multiple machines.
keyPoints: Breaks data into blocks (typically 128MB)
Replicates data across multiple nodes for fault tolerance
Write-once, read-many semantics

title: Apache Spark
description: A unified analytics engine for large-scale data processing, with built-in modules for streaming, SQL, machine learning, and graph processing.
keyPoints: In-memory data processing
Supports multiple languages (Python, Scala, Java, R)
Up to 100x faster than Hadoop MapReduce in memory
pros: Extremely fast due to in-memory processing
Versatile (Batch and Steaming in one framework)
cons: More expensive to run than MapReduce for batch

title: Apache Kafka
description: A distributed event streaming platform used for high-performance data pipelines, streaming analytics, and data integration.
keyPoints: Publish/Subscribe messaging model based on topics
High throughput and extremely low latency
Stores streams of records safely in a fault-tolerant way

title: Batch vs Streaming
description: Different data processing approaches: Batch processes data in large sequential chunks at scheduled intervals, while Streaming processes data continuously as it arrives.
keyPoints: Batch is built for high volume, non-interactive execution
Streaming is built for low latency, continuous execution
Lambda and Kappa architectures often combine or standardize them
useCases: Batch: Payroll processing, Monthly billing reports, Machine learning training
Streaming: Fraud detection, Live system monitoring, Real-time recommendations
pros: Batch: Simpler to implement, handles massive historical volume easily
Streaming: Immediate insights, real-time responsive actions
cons: Batch: Cannot provide immediate answers (high latency)
Streaming: Harder to handle late/out-of-order data, complex state management

title: SQL vs NoSQL
description: SQL databases are relational with a predefined schema and ACID guarantees. NoSQL databases are non-relational, providing flexible schemas for unstructured data at vast scale.
keyPoints: SQL enforces referential integrity and structured schemas
NoSQL scales horizontally easily across distributed clusters
NoSQL types include document, key-value, graph, and wide-column
useCases: SQL: Financial transactions, ERP systems, strict schema requirements
NoSQL: E-commerce catalogs, real-time big data feeds, varied schema storage
pros: SQL: Mature, standardized querying, strong consistency
NoSQL: Highly flexible, highly available, optimized for specific access patterns
cons: SQL: Difficult to scale horizontally, rigid schemas
NoSQL: Looser consistency models (BASE), diverse non-standard APIs

title: Data Lake vs Data Warehouse
description: A Data Warehouse stores highly structured, processed data for direct BI querying. A Data Lake stores massive amounts of raw, unstructured, or semi-structured data for future use.
keyPoints: Warehouse: Schema-on-write (data is structured upon entry)
Lake: Schema-on-read (data is interpreted when read)
Modern setups often use a 'Lakehouse' approach bridging the two
useCases: Warehouse: Business Intelligence, Executive reporting dashboards
Lake: Exploratory data science, Machine Learning, raw data archiving
pros: Warehouse: Fast performance for complex queries, high trust in data quality
Lake: Can store any data type (images, logs, JSON), lower storage cost per GB
cons: Warehouse: Expensive and slow to add new data types
Lake: Can turn into an unusable 'data swamp' without strict governance
